justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — California wage transparency as legal requirement (BR-2), Germany works council approval (BR-3), Indonesia as contractor with no legal obligation. (2) Germany works council approval identified as a blocking pre-condition with feature-flag gating (BR-3, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference explicitly identified — employee vs contractor affecting visible payroll fields (BR-5, ubiquitous language entry). (4) Formal escalation present with multiple ambiguity escalations and business_impact noting legal/HR review needs. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — AC-1 through AC-7 are jurisdiction-specific. (6) confidence.level = 52, well under the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Excellent jurisdiction-specific acceptance criteria — separate ACs for California (AC-1, AC-5), Germany (AC-2, AC-3), and Indonesia (AC-4)
Works council approval modeled as a hard gate with feature flag check, not just a mention — includes revocation edge case
Driver classification difference surfaced as both a ubiquitous language term and a business rule (BR-5) with testable assertion about UI misrepresentation risk
Identified the subtle trap: California display is legally mandatory and cannot be removed (BR-2), while Indonesia language must avoid implying employment (edge case)
Business impact correctly identifies phased jurisdiction-specific rollout as necessary due to works council dependency and legal variance
Confidence at 52 with well-reasoned low-confidence areas reflecting genuine legal uncertainty

Escalations focus on ambiguity type rather than including an explicit 'blocking' escalation stating legal and HR must review before implementation proceeds — the requirement is implied across multiple sections but not consolidated into a single clear escalation
